svg.text: MISHNAIC HEBREW
PHONOLOGY — ARAMAIC INFLUENCE
PHONOLOGICAL SHIFTS IN MISHNAIC PERIOD
Biblical /ʿ/ (ayin = voiced pharyngeal): weakening in Mishnaic period
Some distinctions lost: gimel fricative /ɣ/ merged with regular /g/ in West
Aramaic influence: /θ/ (tav fricative) → /t/ in some positions
Result: Mishnaic Hebrew sounds less conservative than Biblical
ARAMAIC CONTACT PHONOLOGY
Hebrew-Aramaic bilingualism common ~500 BCE-500 CE
Aramaic: no shin/sin distinction (merged to /s/)
Hebrew shin /ʃ/ preserved; sin /s/ merged with samekh in some speech
Sheva mobile: Mishnaic more frequent than Biblical (Aramaic pattern)
MISHNAIC VOWEL SYSTEM
Short vs long vowel distinction beginning to erode
Qamatz gadol and qamatz qatan confusions increase in late texts
Reflects transition toward medieval Hebrew pronunciation traditions
ARABIC PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
CLASSICAL ARABIC PRESERVATION
Classical Arabic: more conservative than Mishnaic Hebrew
Preserves /ʿ/ (ayin) strongly; retains emphatic dad /dˀ/, zha /zˀ/
Quran canonization ~650 CE froze Classical Arabic phonology
ARABIC VOWEL SYSTEM
Classical: 3 short (a i u) + 3 long (a i u) strictly maintained
Short vowels often deleted in rapid speech (colloquial)
Word-final short vowels lost in most modern dialects
SEMITIC PHONOLOGICAL COMPARISON
Biblical Hebrew: most conservative Semitic phonology
Mishnaic Hebrew: transitional, Aramaic influence showing
Classical Arabic: highly conservative, Quran-fixed
Both lost Semitic /pʼ/ (ejective p); both retain pharyngeal /ʿ/
PLATE 10b ALT · MISHNAIC HEBREW / ARABIC · PHONOLOGY
